title: Tiny Epic Dungeons - An Abundance of Icons, Decisions, Challenge
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mDDcQa5yCfI

Daniel (@dungeondive) takes on Gamelyn's *Tiny Epic Dungeons* — the rare entry in the *Tiny Epic* line that, for him, actually delivers a dungeon-crawl feel rather than a Euro-cube exercise dressed up as adventure. Played twice, lost twice without reaching Act 2; the difficulty is real.

Two acts on a 7×7 grid: explore the dungeon to find the boss lair (Act 1), then lure the boss across altar spaces to lower its defences and kill it (Act 2). Two parallel timers: a torch that ticks down each turn, and a four-goblin cap — if you'd spawn a fifth and can't, you lose. The threat-management overlay is what makes the game feel less like *Munchkin* and more like co-op *Pandemic* with a dungeon skin.

Two complaints, both about the box rather than the design. Icon density is overwhelming and the in-box reference doesn't decode many of the most important cards — the BGG appendix is required reading. And the plastic hero minis are a clear downgrade from the wooden boss meeples in the add-on; Daniel wants the entire roster reprinted as meeples.

Includes the Small Stories, Potions & Perils, and boss-meeple expansions.

----

If a small-box game needs a community-made appendix to be fully playable, is that a design flaw or just the cost of cramming a full dungeon crawl into one box?
